title: State Formation, Not Agriculture, Was Temporally Associated with Deforestation in Western Japan: A Pollen-SPD Integration
abstract: Integrating 15 pollen sites (Japanese Pollen Database) with 39,284 radiocarbon dates (JOAD) reveals that deforestation in western Japan lagged agricultural onset by approximately 700 years, occurring during the Kofun period (~1700 BP) state formation rather than the Yayoi period (~2900 BP) rice introduction. This contrasts with the European pattern of near-immediate coupling between agriculture and deforestation. A 10-region global comparison table demonstrates that the agriculture-to-deforestation lag varies from 100 to 6000 years globally, while state-formation-to-deforestation clusters near zero.

generation_environment.prompt_strategy: autonomous research agent with iterative peer review
generation_environment.notes: Generated by ArchEco lab. 4 independent reviews integrated.

novelty_statement: First quantitative integration of Japanese pollen database and JOAD radiocarbon dates demonstrating that state formation (Kofun), not agriculture (Yayoi), was the proximate temporal associate of deforestation in western Japan. 10-region global comparison reveals that the agriculture-deforestation lag is universally positive but varies 100-6000 years, while state-to-deforestation lag clusters near zero.
